scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement of Rome writes to the Corinthian church to reinforce ethical and communal standards within the early Christian community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The First Epistle of Clement is traditionally dated to the end of the reign of Domitian, approximately 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author exhorts the community to observe moral laws regarding the treatment of vulnerable members like widows.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The letter was composed during Clement's leadership of the Roman church, specifically around 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement outlines specific social hierarchies and behavioral expectations for the Christian community in Rome and Corinth.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit and the internal evidence of the letter's composition in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the religious education of children as a collective communal responsibility within the church.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Clement wrote this instructional letter during his tenure as Bishop of Rome, circa 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule reflects the domestic and moral constraints placed on women/wives within the early Christian social order.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Epistle is contemporary to the late 1st century CE, specifically 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement addresses the governance and respect due to leaders (presbyters) within the Christian ecclesiastical structure.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text reflects contemporary concerns regarding church order in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author uses scriptural mandates to define the ethical treatment of the poor within the contemporary church.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Clement's floruit and the date of the letter to the Corinthians is 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule uses contemporary labor norms (wages for work) to teach spiritual principles to the community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The letter is a contemporary document from the Roman church dated to 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement is referencing the historical and religious precedents of the Levitical and Judaean systems as established in Hebrew scripture.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim refers to the established hierarchy of the Second Temple period (post-exilic) as a historical model; -450 represents a central date for these priestly regulations.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Humility is presented as a governing virtue and a criterion for divine favor within the Christian community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author writes at his floruit (96 CE) to address current moral conduct in the Church.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes the specific cultic divisions of Israelites (High Priests, Priests, Levites, Laity) as a historical archetype.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule concerns the hereditary and liturgical functions of the Jewish priesthood in the Second Temple era, anchored here to approximately -450.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement refers to the religious limitations placed upon Jewish laity in contrast to the priesthood as a historical lesson for his audience.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The description pertains to the religious governance of Judea during the Second Temple period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This rule describes the centralized sacrificial laws of Jerusalem and the consequences for unauthorized worship in the Jewish tradition.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Clement refers to the past cultic laws of Jerusalem, specifically the centralized worship established and maintained in the Second Temple period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement is specifically addressing the mid-90s CE crisis in Corinth regarding the removal of bishops.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The letter was written in 96 CE to settle a contemporary dispute over episcopal tenure.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text protests the contemporary ejection of bishops, asserting their right to permanent office if they remain blameless.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text documents a specific 1st-century church crisis occuring around 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The hierarchy of presbyters over the laity is a central tenet of the communal order Clement is defending.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule reflects the organizational norms Clement sought to enforce in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement describes a practice within the contemporary Christian community of voluntary servitude for the benefit of others.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Clement cites this as a practice 'among ourselves' during the time of his writing in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'rulers and governors' mentioned refer to the Roman imperial officials to whom Christians were expected to be submissive.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The letter mandates submission to the contemporary Roman authorities during the reign of Domitian/Nerva.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule demands the submission of specific contemporary rebels in Corinth to their local presbyters.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This refers to the immediate disciplinary resolution Clement was attempting to broker in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement theologizes the sovereignty of the Roman Emperor and governors as divinely granted, requiring subject obedience.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule concerns the political legitimacy of the Roman government at the time of the letter's composition in 96 CE.
